coredata.dc:title: Effect of Ti doping on magnetic properties and magnetoresistance in LaSr2Mn2O7
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Feng, J.
Che, P.
Wang, J.P.
Lu, M.F.
Liu, J.F.
Cao, X.Q.
Meng, J.
coredata.dc:description: The effect of Ti substitution for Mn on magnetic and transport properties has been investigated for layered manganese oxides LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7. Titanium doping hampered the canted antiferromagnetic (AFM) exchange at low temperature and their Neel temperature (T N) decreased from 138K (x =0) to 106K (x =0.1). Meanwhile, spin glass, charge ordering and metal-insulator transition are suppressed by Ti addition. This can be attributed to Mn-site disorder caused by random substitution of Ti4+. The suppression of charge ordering leads to magntetoresistance (MR) ratio increase and MR reaches maximum at x =0.3. The resistivity increases obviously with x increasing because of double exchange interaction channel broken by Ti4+ addition. The resistivity of all samples in low temperature range fits to the Mott's variable range hopping (VRH) model, while it fits to nearest neighbor hopping of small polarons model in high temperature range. We also found that both disorder and distortion in A-site and B-site will induce the similar effect to electrical and magnetic properties.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Magnetoresistance
Perovskite
Charge order
Double exchange

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: X-ray diffraction patterns of LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7 (x =0–0.3).
(a) The magnetization dependent temperature of LaSr2Mn2O7 under applied fields of 0.05 and 0.3T. (b) The ZFC–FC curve for x =0.05, 0.1, 0.3.
Resistivity depends on temperature for LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7.
Resistivity in low temperature fitting to VRH rule.
Resistivity in high temperature fitting to SPH rule.
MR ratio depends on temperature for all samples.
Lattice parameter, T CO and T N varied with Ti content
Resistivity fitting parameter varied with Ti content

article.ce:title: Effect of Ti doping on magnetic properties and magnetoresistance in LaSr2Mn2O7

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Chemistry and Physics, Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Changchun, 130022 Jilin, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The effect of Ti substitution for Mn on magnetic and transport properties has been investigated for layered manganese oxides LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7. Titanium doping hampered the canted antiferromagnetic (AFM) exchange at low temperature and their Neel temperature (T N) decreased from 138K (x =0) to 106K (x =0.1). Meanwhile, spin glass, charge ordering and metal-insulator transition are suppressed by Ti addition. This can be attributed to Mn-site disorder caused by random substitution of Ti4+. The suppression of charge ordering leads to magntetoresistance (MR) ratio increase and MR reaches maximum at x =0.3. The resistivity increases obviously with x increasing because of double exchange interaction channel broken by Ti4+ addition. The resistivity of all samples in low temperature range fits to the Mott's variable range hopping (VRH) model, while it fits to nearest neighbor hopping of small polarons model in high temperature range. We also found that both disorder and distortion in A-site and B-site will induce the similar effect to electrical and magnetic properties.

ce:section.ce:para: The discovery of colossal magnetoresistance (CMR) in doped rare-earth manganese perovskite oxides has aroused much interest in recent years [1,2]. It is well known that the common formula of perovskite phase is (AO)(ABO3) n , where A is lanthanide or alkaline-earth metals, B is transition metal, n is the dimension. Now double-exchange (DE) interaction between Mn4+/Mn3+ pairs, electron–phonon couple arose from Jahn-Teller (JT) effect and orbital ordering effect are commonly adopted as the theoretical main ingredients [3,4]. It is well known that Mn3+ is necessary for DE and JT effect, so perovskite phase need vary valence cation substitution (e.g. La3+ for Ca2+) to generate a mixed-valence Mn3+/Mn4+ array. As we shall see the mixed valent state and its generation of mobile carriers are critical to understanding the behavior of perovskite manganite. The DE mechanism links the electronic to the magnetic transition and describes the hopping of electrons in eg orbitals between neighboring Mn3+ and Mn4+ sites with strong on-site Hund's coupling by O2− ions [4].

The n = ∞ manganate with formula A1−x T x MnO3 (A is rare-earth element and T is alkaline-earth metal) is studied widely for their high Curie-temperature and large MR property [5]. Recently, MR effect was also found in the n =2 perovskite manganate compounds with formula A2−2x T2x Mn2O7 compounds [6,7], in which the MnO2 bilayers and the rock salt layers are stacked vertically along the c-axis. The n = ∞ and n =2 phase, however, both show a variety of magnetic and electronic states. In the system of La2−2x Sr2x Mn2O7, taking the Mn3+-based compound La2SrMn2O7 (x =0) and the Mn4+-based compound Sr3Mn2O7 (x =1) as the parent compounds [8,9], the interesting ferromagnetic (FM) metal narrow range is from x =0.3 to x =0.4. Otherwise, the system of La2−2x Sr2x Mn2O7 is very sensitive to Sr content. For instance, when x changes from 0.3 to 0.32, there is a transition from AFM to FM. The same change also occurs at x changing from 0.45 to 0.48 [10,11]. The middle compound LaSr2Mn2O7 (x =0.5) shows antiferromagnetic (Mn3+ and Mn4+ pair are AFM couples with their nearest neighboring called CE-type) charge ordering (CO) below 210K and below 140K it exists AFM spin couple [12]. CO has been observed in several CE-type AFM half-doped manganites, such as La0.5Ca0.5MnO3 [13], Sm0.5Ca0.5MnO3 [14], Bi0.5Sr0.5MnO3 [15]. Meanwhile, it is important for understanding the physical properties. Beside the aim of doping for mix valence of Mn3+ and Mn4+, the A-site disorder induced by A-site doping in La1.2Sr1.8Mn2O7 and the B-site disorder caused by Mn-site disordering also have been studied by several groups [16–19].

In the present paper, the results of the magnetic and transport behavior of Mn-site layered perovskite LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7 (x =0–0.3) are reported. In the magnetic phase graph of La2−2x Sr2x Mn2O7, the half-doped is the critical boundary of A-type AFM and canted AFM [12]. We choose half-doped system in order to investigate the difference between A-site doped and Mn-doped. Choosing Ti ion is because Ti4+ can substitute Mn4+ directly and the ionic radius of Ti4+ is comparable with that of Mn3+. In addition, the effect of doping Ti in La1−x Pb x Mn1−y Ti y O3, in which the ferromagnetic and magnetoresistance characteristic are sensitive to Ti doping, has been reported in an earlier publication [18]. Therefore, we can compare the difference of Mn-site disorder in n = ∞ and n =2 systems. The effects of Ti doping on charge ordering and resistivity transition are also discussed.

The bulk samples of LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7 (x =0–0.3) were prepared by standard ceramic process. Stoichiometric amounts of high purity powder of La2O3, SrCO3, Mn2O3, and TiO2 were mixed with a pre-treatment in order to wipe out water, ground and calcined at 800°C for 12h and 1000°C for 24h in air. The black powders were well ground again, then pelletized and sintered at 1400°C for 72h slowly cooling to room temperature with intermediate grindings.

To characterize the samples, X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for all the samples were recorded on a Rigaku D/max-IIB X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation (λ =1.5405Å). The electric resistivity was measured by a standard four-probe method. The temperature dependence of magnetization from 80 to 295K was collected on a VSM-7300 vibrating sample magnetometer (Lakeshore, USA). The measurements were taken in both zero-field-cooled (ZFC) and field-cooled (FC) conditions under applied field of 500Oe. All the data were taken in the warming run.

The X-ray diffraction patterns for the series LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7 (x =0–0.3) are presented in Fig. 1 . From Fig. 1, it is clear that in the range of study all the diffraction peaks are indexed with respect to the Sr3Ti2O7-type perovskite, space group is I4/mmm. The calculated unit-cell parameters are listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that both the unit-cell parameters a and c show a linear increase with Ti content increasing. This indicates that they obey the Vegard's law, which means the unit cell parameters are a linear function of the cation ionic radii on the A or B sites, while the radii of Ti4+ (0.68Å) are larger than Mn3+ (0.66Å) and Mn4+ (0.60Å).

Fig. 2 shows the curves of the magnetization dependence of temperature for LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7. In LaSr2Mn2O7, it was reported that there is CE-type charge ordering below 210K and antiferromagnetic spin couple below 140K [12]. From Fig. 2, it is seen that the temperature of charge ordering (T CO) is 190 and 210K measured in 3000 and 500Oe, respectively. T CO increasing with field decreasing indicates that it relates to some AFM couple of spin. Otherwise, the discrepancy of ZFC and FC occurs at about 200K. The discrepancy decreasing with the field increasing means that there is an AFM interaction competing with the dominant double exchange ferromagnetic interaction. As shown in Fig. 2b, the ZFC and FC curves show discrepancies for all samples even if x =0.3, but the discrepancy and T CO decrease with the x value. Ti4+ doping lead to the T CO decreasing from 210K of x =0–169K of x =0.1 and disappeared subsequently. This phenomenon implies that the Ti4+ substitutions suppressed spin glass state and charge ordering. In addition, the canted AFM exchange is only found in x ≤0.1 samples and T N decreases from 138K (x =0) to 106K (x =0.1). This suggests that the canted AFM exchange was hampered by Ti doping at low temperature. This is similar in that T c is decreased by Ti doped in (La1−x Pb x )Mn1−y Ti y O3 [18] and La2/3Ca1/3Mn1−x Al x O3 [20]. This result can be ascribed to the disorder induced by random substitution of Ti4+ in manganese sublattice. However, in LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7 the magnetic moments increase and reach maximum at x =0.1 at low temperature. But in (La1−x Pb x )Mn1−y Ti y O3 system the magnetism moment decreased with Ti content and still kept FM in the range of y ≤0.2, which was thought to be the ferromagnetic superexchange interaction of Mn3+ O2− Mn3+. Based on these results, it is suggested that there are more complex interaction in Mn sites doping for the two layers perovskite of (RE,M)3Mn2O7 compared with the three-dimensional perovskite (RE,M)MnO3. It was the reason that the bilayers MnO6 octahedron are separated by La/SrO rock-salt layers, so that the interaction between interbilayer along c-axis is weaker than that in the intralayer direction, considering the character of n =2 perovskite is two-dimensional and the anisotropism of the MnOMn net. Meanwhile, the parent sample is CE-type magnetic structure (interlayer is FM and intrabilayer couple is AFM). So when Ti4+ substitute for Mn4+ directly, it partly breaks AFM of intrabilayer and remain short range FM order, even in the heavy doping. However, the replacement of Mn4+ by Ti4+ will decrease the number of available orbital, and hampers the double exchange interaction between the Mn3+ and Mn4+. Thereupon, n = ∞ perovskite is very sensitive to doping Ti compared to n =2 perovskite.

The resistivity dependence of temperature is given in Fig. 3 . From Fig. 3, it is obvious that the resistivities dramatically increase with x increasing. This is the reason for increasing the B-site disorder and breaking the electric transport channels induced by the Ti4+ addition. Ti doping reduces the number of empty orbital of Mn4+, and hence, double exchange interaction channel is broken. For undoped sample, there is a metal-insulator (MI) transition near 140K, but the MI transition disappeared even slightly doped (x =0.05). It suggests that the MI transition of doped samples may be shifted to lower temperature that is lower than our test limit. According to Mott's variable range hopping (VRH) rule [21], the resistivity in low temperature (T <200K) can be written as follows:
ρ =
ρ 0
exp
(
T 0
/ T )
− 1 / 4
where ρ 0 is the pre-factor and T 0 represents the characteristic temperature. Fig. 4 shows the curves of resistance fitting in low temperature.

A fit was made between resistivity and temperature according to the Mott's VRH rule. The fitting parameters are listed in Table 2 . Electron hopping is always variable-range type at low temperature where the thermal energy is not great enough to allow electrons to hop to the nearest neighbors [21]. In low temperature range the increasing disorder in Mn-site makes random spin and Coulombic interaction, so the carrier finds some potential difference orbital beyond the MnO distances to hop at farther distance and hence conduct through the Mott's type of VRH [22]. The characteristic temperature T 0 can be related to the carrier localization length by the expression:
κ
T 0
= 24 /
L 3
π N ( E )
where κ is the Boltzmann constant, L is the carrier localization and the N(E) is density of state [22–24]. The value of the localization length should be comparable to MnO distance for VRH type of conduction. If the L =10−10 m3 [25] was taken the density of state N(E) will be given (see Table 2). It is two or three orders of magnitude higher than similar compound. From Table 2, it is seen that the N(E) value gradually decreases with x. This means that the conductivity gradually decreased with Ti doping. For the change of resistivity in high temperature (T >200K) in LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7, the relationship between resistivity and temperature obey the nearest neighbor hopping of small polarons (NSPH) [23]:
ρ / T =
ρ 0
exp (
E p
/
K B
T )
where E p is the characteristic energy for polar hopping. Fig. 5 shows the curves of resistance fitting in high temperature. From Table 2, it is obvious that the activation energy (E p) gradually increases with increasing x. It is obvious that with the increasing of Ti concentration the number of empty orbits of Mn4+ became fewer, which leads to an increase in the activation energy.

The MR ratio as the function of temperature of LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7 for x =0–0.3 are plotted in Fig. 6 . The MR values of different temperature were calculated using the expression: MR=100%×[(ρ(0)− ρ(1.7T))/ρ(0)], where ρ(0) and ρ(H) are the values of the samples for zero magnetic and magnetic field, respectively. The MR have, however, all largely increased, especially in the sample of x =0.3 (see Fig. 6). This is the same in the cases of Mn-site doped by Cr, Ni and Fe transition-metal ions [26]. It is thought that the ferromagnetic and CO state are suppressed entirely at x =0.3. For x =0 and x =0.3, the compound LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7 has a maximum MR ratio at about 160K and a negative minimum MR value at about 115K, which is close to the T CO and T N of this system, respectively.

However, the Ti substitution for Mn ion shows the same result with the A-site doped with La2−2x Sr2x Mn2O7. Y. Moritomo and coworkers [16,17] have proposed that the substitution in A-site in La1.2−x Nd x Sr1.8Mn2O7 and La1.2Sr1.8−x Ca x Mn2O7, the doping in A-site increases the disorder and leads to the FM state in the system. The T c and M–I transition are suppressed but the MR ratio increased with doping content x and the temperature of MR maximal T MR decreased with x [16,17]. For LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7 system, with the doping of Ti, the T CO, T N and M–I transition are also suppressed and the MR increase. Thereupon, it suggests that both disorder and distortion in A-site and B-site will lead to the similar effect to electrical and magnetic properties.

In summary, the LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7 (x =0–0.3) compound was synthesized and their electrical, magnetic, colossal magnetoresistance properties were investigated. The relation between resistivity and temperature obeys Mott's variable range hopping (VRH) rule in low temperature, while it fits to nearest neighbor hopping of small polarons in higher temperature range. Ti doping suppressed the canted antiferromagnetic couple and T CO, T N and M–I transition are also suppressed, but the MR increases. Also, the n =2 perovskite is very sensitive to doping Ti compared to the n = ∞ perovskite. The results described above indicate that Ti doping can weaken the charge ordering and metallic conduction of LaSr2Mn2−x Ti x O7. These results can be attributed to the disorder in B-site and the breakage of double exchange channel in the Mn3+ O2− Mn4+. It indicates that both disorder and distortion in A-site and B-site will induce the similar effect to electrical and magnetic properties.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Nature Scientific Foundation of China (20331030, 20271049).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Proceedings of the 15th Pacific Rim International Conference on Advanced Materials
